speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, October 26, 1995. We're speaking with Mr. Sam Zelikson. Mr. Zelikson, you were describing your experiences in the camp Stutthof. Could you continue, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were working as slaves. We hardly got enough food just to survive. And we had to work from 6 o'clock to around daylight whenever we could, like 8, 9 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you were there for six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So during that six month period-- during that six month period, do you recall remembering that it was Passover in April or Purim in March?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any way of knowing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear anyone mention-- did word go around the camp that it was a particular holiday?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE], I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: --four?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So should I say that or just let you do your think?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just go ahead and just pick up a question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. OK. Mr. Zelikson, you were discussing your experiences at Stutthof--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and witnessing the barbaric act of the Germans feeding two of the prisoners to dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the other significant experiences that you had while in Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I met my adopted brother there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin Kulbak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were on the same block. We went out to work together. And he, at that time, was sick. And he couldn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told the German that I'm going to do my work and his work while he's sick. He says, if you can do it, it's OK. He was not a Nazi, he was a Wehrmacht. So he said, it's OK as long I do both the work. So I did both work. I had to do my work and do his work. And did Martin get better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin is one year younger than me. So at that time, I was what, 13? So he-- he was 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were some of the other experiences you had at Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not much experience. We was thinking every day where we're going to go next. At the next camp, before we knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Neuengamme.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neuengamme, that was end of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how were you transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning of '43, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: With trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Stutthof for a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stutthof we were six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you don't mind repeating--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you brought to Neuengamme?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said beginning of '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: In beginning of '43. Must have been in March.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were taken by trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken by trucks. They brought us in Neuengamme. In Neuengamme, we had to do all kinds of work. But the work, the regular work, we had to do the same thing like we did in Stutthof, dig ditches, chop wood. But then, at night, they give us small work. We worked till-- from 6 o'clock in the morning to about 7 o'clock at night. 8 o'clock, they picked out-- we were 14 workers, actually kids, young people. And they took us to a place in the camp, but in back of the camp. And there was a lot of children [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dead, laying. And they told us to cut off the limbs, cut off the arms, the legs, and put-- to put the stuff in a big barrel. There was about four barrels, five barrels. And we had to put it in. And then we had to put some kind of a chemical. We didn't know what chemical it was. Every time we put in the limbs, we had to put in the chemical. Like, I guess it shouldn't smell, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I says to one of the guards, what are we doing with this? He says, we're sending it to Germany. They're making soap. Were you wearing gloves or a mask?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at all. No mask. No gloves. It had a terrible smell, but we had to endure it. And the next day, there were a lot of women. And they gave us big shears. And each one of us had to cut the women's hair off and put it also in-- in big baskets, the hair. So we had to take all the women's, their hair, cut off. And then we saw another site. There was a lot of dead women. And the German says, cut the breast off. And they put it in another barrel and put some other stuff. We had to put it on. And he called us in. And he told us to swear that, if we ever say anything or tell anybody, we're going to wind up the same like these people wind up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was after--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to have a secrecy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your hours, again, to do this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were working from 8 o'clock to 3 o'clock in the morning. 6 o'clock in the morning, we had to go. So we had maybe two, three hours to sleep. We had to go to do the slave labor in Neuengamme.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from 6 o'clock in the morning till--

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 7 o'clock at night, we worked the slave labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: At 8 o'clock, they took us to do the brutal work. It's indescribable. It's-- even now, my body shivers to tell the story like this. Unfortunately, it's true. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's indescribable what we had to do. And don't forget, we was children. We weren't grown up. We were 13, 14 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did this last? How long were you in Neuengamme?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Neuengamme? We were almost a year in Neuengamme.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this type of work, is this what your routine was for a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It kept on going. And every time they ran out of bodies, they brought more. I don't know where they took it from, but there was plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your friend get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: After doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But after a while, you got used to it. We didn't want it, but we had no choice. If you refused, they would hit us or sic the dog on us. And they didn't care. If they kill us, they would get more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after Neuengamme, they sent us to Aurich. Aurich, that was a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you spell Aurich? Please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aurich? A-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: --have to this tape, because I'm a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're at nine minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. Mr. Zelikson, you were discussing your difficult experiences in Neuengamme.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were transferred to Aurick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aurich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aurich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Aurich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aurich, it's in Germany. It-- it would be north Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, it's no longer Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Aurich is still Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you transferred from Neuengamme to Aurich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trucks at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you with your friend, Martin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still had your clothing from Neuengamme? And what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Striped. The striped clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Striped clothes and the cap--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with the 238 number on your shirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- did you have shoes, still, at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not-- not shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sandals?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was actually not shoes. It-- it was made out of rags. They gave us-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it looked like sandals. But it was made out of rags, not out of leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like when you got off the truck to Aurich?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went in. Over there was nice. There was no more SS. It was the Wehrmacht. And they gave us good food there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do call good food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they give us bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They gave us bread. They gave us soup. They fed us pretty good. They didn't hit us. We had to dig ditches, so a tank would fall in. We had big ditches we had to dig. And my adopted brother, my friend, he started to dig the ditches, and he got sick. So I pushed him away. And I says, I'm going to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was not the Wehrmacht-- the prisoner that was in charge of prisoners. So he came over, and he hit me. He says, why you doing his work? This was a German prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was a Ukranian. Ukranian. He was in charge. He was like a foreman, a kapo. And he says, why are you doing his work? I says, he don't feel good. I have to do it. He says, I'm going to hit you and he started hitting me. Then I says, I'm still going to do it. He kept on hitting me more. And blood came out all over my face, out from my nose. And I smeared my face with the blood all over. So he says, wash up after you finish work. I says, no, I'm not going to wash up. He says, if you don't wash up, I'll hit you more. I says, I don't care if you kill me. I'm not going to wash.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to the camp. They took us with trucks to dig the ditches. And they took us back with the trucks to camp. And when I came and the commander, he says, what happened to you? And I told him that I was digging the ditch for my friend, because he couldn't do it. And he told me, that's why he hit you? I say, yeah. That's why he hit me. I did my work, and I did my brother's work. So he called in the foreman. And he said, did you do this to him? And he says, yes, because he didn't do any work. So the commander, he was above the leutnant. That's like a lieutenant. So he says to him, that's not what he told me. You hit him for no reason, because he did his work. And he called in another soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told him to beat him up like he beat me up. And after that, the guy didn't want to even talk to me. He was mad at me. But that was already about the close to the middle of 1944. We finished the work. And they put us all in a freight train. And in freight train, they only give us water. They didn't give us food for six days, just a little cup of water. And they told us they're going to send us to Bergen-Belsen. And a lot of people were dead in the car where I was. And it was very cold. We used to take the bodies and cover ourselves with the dead people so to get warm. And like I said, we didn't eat for six days. And then I saw a German, like a miracle happened. A German took out a big loaf of bread. It was about 4 inches, maybe, by 8 inches. And he put it right by the door of the freight train. And he went to relieve himself. I jumped out. And I grabbed the loaf of bread. And I wanted to eat the loaf of bread, because I was very hungry. But there was 200 people in the train. And I couldn't eat it. I took the loaf of bread. And I broke up for 200 pieces. And I gave everybody a piece and kept one for me. And then my adopted brother that is in Canada now, he looked at me with these eyes. And he was very sick. And I looked at the piece of bread, and I looked at his eyes. And I couldn't eat the bread. I gave him my piece of bread. And because of the piece of bread, maybe he survived. That's why we love each other so much. And they brought us to Bergen-Belsen. And Bergen-Belsen, that was a real extermination camp. We came with the train to Bergen-Belsen, and there was a lot of bodies laying, maybe thousands. And they told us to dig ditch and put in the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a uniform when you got off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had the same uniform--

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the striped uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Neuengamme?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they give me a number, 1638, in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave you a new number?

speaker: interviewee
text: A new number. And we had to bury the-- the people were naked. They were laying, women, men. There was no children. Old people, older people and young girls, young guys. They were laying dead. I don't know from what they died, but they were dead. And we had to bury them. And there was plenty to bury. And the commander from the Bergen-Belsen was Josef Krempf-- Kramer. Josef Kramer. And one night, we saw there was no SS. We only saw old German soldiers. And everybody says, the war was over. I said, how can be the war-- there was the German soldiers. And we started to move toward them. They says, don't go. We're going to shoot you. And then we find out that they didn't even have bullets in the rifles. And we started to move. And then we heard the sirens and the trucks and tanks. And the England are coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: The English?

speaker: interviewee
text: The English came in. And at that time, I was sick with typhus. So everybody that was sick, the English guy that was in charge, he had a marker. And he put a big, black cross on my forehead. And whoever had a black cross on the forehead, they took him to ambulances to the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the end of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, must have been about two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you were sick with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Typhus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, they transferred it to Bergen. It's also Bergen-Belsen. But it was the place where the Nazis used to have their quarters. The military quarters, they put us in. And I was in block 25. And they asked who has knowledge of firearms, because there was a lot of people. And it was a lot of commotion going on. And they wanted to have control.

speaker: interviewer
text: They says, who wants to volunteer for police department? Of course, like always, I had volunteered. And I became a policeman. And I was a policeman till 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to meet two friends of mine. And we went to Berlin to see if maybe one of our relatives survived, to find out. I saw a Russian. And he started to talk to me in Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: I answered him back. He said, you speak Russian? I say, yeah, I speak Russian. He says, where did you learn? I told him in school. He says, you come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me. And I saw it was-- I was in the headquarters. And the sergeant says-- a politruk, at that time, it was politruks-- and he says, you're now a Russian soldier. You stay here. I says, I'm looking for my relatives, see. I-- I don't want to be a soldier. He says, you have no choice. You go and stay here. I stayed for six months. And then I said to my commander, I says, I'm going to look for my relatives, see if I have somebody. He said, I'll give you a three day pass. I says, it's like a needle in a haystack. I can't do it. He said, that's all I can give you, three days. So he gave me three days, and I took another three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't find nobody. Everybody perished from-- we were a family of 90 people and everybody perished. So I start to go back. A friend of mine saw me. He said, Shashka. At that time, they called me instead of [INAUDIBLE], they called me Shashka in Russian-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I'm going back to my division. He says, don't. Because they moved out to Moscow. And you're going to be shot if you come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with my uniform, with my gun. And we went in a farm. And I says, listen. Give me your clothes, whatever you have. I have to have the clothes. So he didn't want it. I took out the gun and says, you better give me the clothes. So he gave me the clothes. I said, here. You could have the gun. You could have my uniform. Bury it. And I took it, and I went back to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I says to-- my commander was Wernick from the police department. I says, look. I went to look for my relative. And I got held up with the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put me in the Russian army. I'm still going to be a policeman? He said, yes. But then he says to me, they're looking who wants to go to the United States, to move to United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I would love to. I wrote it on a piece of paper. And I waited. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before going to the United States, I belonged to the Haganah, that was an Israel organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they say, listen. You want to go to Israel? I said, sure. I want to go Israel. But you've got to fight. I says, I'll go fight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us, we were a couple hundred people. They took us with a truck to a ship. And we went to a ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Bremerhaven. And from Bermerhaven, we went to a ship to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the ship? What was the name of this ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We called it Shalom, but it wasn't. It was a German name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I can't remember. I might have it in my transcript. I'll remember. We went with the ship. And just maybe about 50 miles from Israel, an English, big destroyer caught us. And they shot over the ship. And they told us to stop, which the captain stopped the ship. And they came over with the boat. And they said, they can't go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us to Cypress. And we stayed there for about a month. It was a camp there. And they sent us back to Germany again. They told them we can't go to Israel. They sent us back to Germany. And you went back to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the I went to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go back to in Germany? Where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1950. January 28.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it take you long to get a visa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. You didn't need a visa. You had to sign a paper. You're a displaced person. And they sent us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1950, you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to the United States. And the HIAS brought us over. And they were giving us a little money to live on. But I told them, I don't want a charity. I wanted a job. I don't want to get paid charity. Whatever I get, I want to earn. They says, OK. They'll give me a job. There was a butcher-- and they find out I was a butcher-- market. It was on Pitkin Avenue and 95th Street. I came to the butcher to work. I worked for him. I did everything, cut the meat, pluck the chicken. The women used to give me $0.10 when I plucked the chicken. I used to do maybe 30, 40 chickens a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn. And at night, I wanted to take the money. The woman says, you can't take it. That belongs to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The butcher's wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: The butcher's wife. So I told the women, I said not to leave me money, because she's taking it away. So they didn't want it. And they got very mad. I worked for a month. I worked maybe 14, 12, 14 hours a day. And he was paying me $25 a week. So I says to him, listen. I can't make a living. I can't exist. Give me $10 a raise. So he says, I'll give you $5. I says, no. $10. And then he started haggling. I don't like haggling. I told him, forget it. Give me the money, and I'll go someplace else to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, he can't get me because he needs the accountant. And he'll make out a check. And that was Friday. And the accountant is coming in Monday. I says, listen. You just took in $700. Give me $25. So, oh, you count my money? I say, I didn't count. I know what you got.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says he's not going to give to me. I said then somebody else will come in and collect. So he got mad at me, and he took the knife sharpener. That's a big iron. We call it in Russian. And he went after me. He was in the 70s. So I says to him, look. Put this away before I break your arm. And he looked at me, and he put away. And he went, and he picked up the butcher knife. I grabbed him by his chest. And luckily, he was wearing a pelt, because he was going in the freezer. And you had to have warm clothes. So I picked him up, and I hung him right on the hook. And the woman started to scream and called police. And the police came. And they put me in handcuffs, my hands behind my back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And luckily, the sergeant was Jewish. And he told me his name is [INAUDIBLE]. And I says to him, I came to the United States. And now you put me in handcuffs? For what? I didn't do anything. He took the knife to me. He says, listen. I have a friend of mine. He's a judge, Judge Leibowitz. He lives on Pitkin Avenue. I says, you'll call-- call up the judge and see what he can do? So he called up, and the judge says to bring me right over.
